text: The patient is positioned supine with legs raised in orthotomy. Surgical ports are placed according to a displayed diagram. The patient is then positioned into reverse Trendelenburg with the right shoulder tilted down, facilitating the movement of the small bowel and omentum into the right and upper abdomen, thereby exposing the relevant anatomical structures for the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the groove of the inferior mesenteric artery and makes an initial incision in the peritoneum at the level of the pelvic brim. Medial to lateral dissection is performed, taking care to avoid the ureter and hypogastric nerves. The inferior mesenteric artery is identified and skeletalized, with Hemlock clips applied before dividing the vessel approximately one centimeter from its origin.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, separating retroperitoneal structures from the mesocolon. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified and divided at the lower border of the pancreas, with a window created over the pancreas to access the lesser sac. A swab is placed as a landmark for splenic flexure mobilization, followed by further dissection towards the lateral wall. A second swab is placed to mark lateral mobilization, utilizing traction and counter traction techniques.

The surgeon begins splenic flexure mobilization by separating the gastrocolic ligament to enter the lesser sac, continuing laterally until reaching the splenic flexure. The splenocolic ligament is then divided, with a swab visible posteriorly over the pancreas serving as a landmark. The mobilization unites with the previously created window over the pancreas, with the stomach visible nearby.
The surgeon uses counter traction to straighten the splenic flexure, uniting the superior, lateral, and posterior portions of the dissection to achieve full mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision using monopolar hook diathermy. The rectum is visualized as a four-sided box, and the posterior dissection is initiated above the presacral fascia, extending towards the pelvic floor. Care is taken to avoid injury to the presacral vessels. Once the posterior dissection is maximized, lateral mobilization begins two centimeters above the peritoneal reflection, emphasizing traction and counter traction. The lateral dissection is then united anteriorly. The uterus is elevated using a two O proline stitch.

The surgeon begins the anterior dissection using a monopolar hook diathermy, carefully avoiding injury to the posterior vaginal wall. The tool allows for precise dissection, with optimum traction and counter traction being essential.

Following the initial circumferential mobilization, the surgeon proceeds with deeper dissection towards the pelvic floor. The lateral pelvic nerves are visible, and care is taken to avoid injuring them during the lateral mobilization.

The surgeon manipulates the rectum to free any remaining mesorectal tissue. The rectal tube is transected using an Echelon 45 stapler, requiring two firings. After removal of the rectum, the surrounding pelvic structures are clearly visible.

The surgeon forms the anastomosis using a CDH-XXIX circular stapling device, with the trocar visibly emerging from the midpoint of the staple line running anterior to posterior. The specimen is extracted through a small midline incision. The bowel is checked for correct orientation and tension in the pelvis before firing the stapler. A flexible sigmoidoscope is used to perform a leak test to verify the integrity of the anastomosis. A loop ileostomy is created, and a pelvic drain is inserted.
